Rolling Analysis (52-Week Window)
Rich / Cheap Analysis
Methodology: Cumulative OLS residuals measure COPX's total return vs. what the factor model predicts. Positive = COPX has outperformed model (expensive). Negative = underperformed (cheap). Z-score normalizes over a 2-year rolling window for regime-adaptive signals.
Kalman Filter: Time-Varying Betas
Model: COPX_t = x_t' β_t + ε_t  |  β_t = β_{t-1} + η_t (random-walk coefficients)
MLE Hyperparameters: σ²_obs = 5.0563  |  σ²_state = 0.001753  |  Log-Likelihood = -1965.1
Smoothed-fit R² = 0.8275 (vs. static OLS R² = 0.7611). Allowing betas to drift time-varyingly explains +6.6 pp more of COPX's variance.

Betas: Current Kalman (Smoothed) vs Long-Run OLS

CopperEEMTLTUSDSPY
Kalman β (latest)+0.784+0.792+0.574-0.969+0.313
OLS β (full sample)+0.728+0.603-0.194-0.863+0.302
Δ (Kalman − OLS)+0.056+0.189+0.768-0.106+0.011
Interpretation: The Kalman filter treats each β_t as a latent state evolving smoothly over time. The smoothed beta uses the full sample (best in-sample estimate). The one-step-ahead innovation uses only information available up to t−1, so it's the honest real-time "surprise" vs. what the filter would have predicted — a proper out-of-sample residual at every point. The innovation-based rich/cheap signal is therefore more faithful than the static OLS residual signal above.
